rows.title: Cyber Security Agency of Singapore and Japan's METI Sign Mutual Recognition Memorandum of Cooperation for IoT Cybersecurity Labelling Schemes
rows.summary:
The Cyber Security Agency of Singapore and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry have entered into a Memorandum of Cooperation providing for mutual recognition of their respective IoT cybersecurity labelling schemes, with Japan becoming the fifth country to hold such an arrangement with Singapore.

On 18 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA) and Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI) signed the Memorandum of Cooperation (MoC), formalising mutual recognition between Singapore's Cybersecurity Labelling Scheme (CLS) and Japan's Japan Cyber STAR (JC-STAR) scheme. The agreement was signed by Ms Rahayu Mahzam, Minister of State for Digital Development and Information, and Mr Ino Toshiro, State Minister of Economy, Trade and Industry of Japan. CSA states that no financial penalties are associated with this instrument.

CSA notes that under the arrangement, smart devices that have obtained cybersecurity labels under either the CLS or JC-STAR will be mutually recognised across both jurisdictions, allowing manufacturers to apply for the other country's labelling scheme through a streamlined process. The CSA confirms that the arrangement applies to a range of smart devices, including smart home assistants, home automation and alarm systems, IoT gateways, and hubs that connect multiple devices. CSA states that the MoC is to take effect on 1 June 2026.

CSA describes the CLS as a voluntary scheme, with a mandatory exception for Wi-Fi routers, for which obtaining CLS Level 1 is required. Under the CLS, CSA notes that smart devices are rated according to their levels of cybersecurity provisions, with ratings displayed as one to four stars, each additional star indicating progressively more rigorous testing and security assessments. CSA states that since the CLS launched in 2020, it has received applications for over 1,000 products, ranging from routers to smart lighting to smart cameras.

CSA confirms that Japan is the fifth nation to enter into such a mutual recognition arrangement with Singapore, following Finland, Germany, the Republic of Korea, and the United Kingdom. The CSA notes, however, that Finland discontinued its own cybersecurity labelling scheme on 31 July 2025, following the mutual recognition arrangement established with Singapore in 2021. The CSA states that the cooperation is intended to enhance cybersecurity standards for IoT devices across both nations, provide market access for manufacturers, and give consumers greater confidence in the security of their smart devices.

rows.title: CSA Mandates Cyber Trust Mark Certification for Critical Information Infrastructure Owners, Auditors and Licensed Cybersecurity Providers
rows.summary:
The Cyber Security Agency of Singapore has announced mandatory Cyber Trust Mark certification requirements for Critical Information Infrastructure Owners, CII auditors, and licensed cybersecurity service providers, with tiered deadlines running to end-2027, under the Cybersecurity Act (s. 49).

On 2 March 2026, the Cyber Security Agency of Singapore (CSA) announced, at the Ministry of Digital Development and Information Committee of Supply Debates 2026, that Critical Information Infrastructure Owners (CIIOs), approved auditors conducting audits for CIIOs, and licensed cybersecurity service providers engaged in penetration testing and managed security operations centre monitoring services are required to obtain certification under the Cyber Trust Mark (CTM) framework. No financial penalties were announced in connection with this requirement; the announcement concerns certification obligations rather than enforcement action.

CSA states that CIIOs are required to attain CTM Level 5, the highest tier of the five-tier certification framework, for non-CII systems under their control that support business operations and services. CSA notes that CIIOs are granted a two-year grace period, with a compliance deadline of end-2027. CSA states that CII auditors are required to obtain CTM Level 5 at the organisational level for systems supporting their business operations, with a one-year grace period ending by end-2026. CSA notes that the CTM framework was enhanced in a prior period to incorporate domains covering Cloud security, Operational Technology security, and Artificial Intelligence security, reflecting what the agency describes as a fast-evolving digital landscape. The framework comprises five cybersecurity preparedness tiers, each encompassing between 10 and 22 domains.

For licensed cybersecurity service providers, CSA states that the requirement to obtain CTM Promoter (Tier 3) certification was implemented in February 2026 following a four-week public consultation conducted from 22 September 2025 to 21 October 2025. CSA received 17 responses to that consultation, with the agency noting that the majority of respondents welcomed the proposal to raise baseline cyber hygiene for providers with access to client data and systems. CSA finds that the measure is intended to reduce risks arising from cyber supply chain interdependencies. Licensed providers are granted a grace period until 31 December 2026 to obtain the required certification under s. 49 of the Cybersecurity Act, which governs the licensing of penetration testing and managed security operations centre monitoring services in Singapore.
